rationale: Dynamic authority decay and runtime legitimacy mechanisms for agentic AI are structurally transferable to AI coding agent permission management; legal framing wraps an operational pattern (time-limited authorizations requiring re-validation).

Clarifies the compliance trigger point (when fine-tuning makes you a GPAI provider) that software orgs deploying modified coding models must operationally plan around; knowing WHEN obligations attach is itself actionable.

Regulatory strategy analysis arguing what approaches 'are unlikely to be strong candidates' — this is policy evaluation for regulators, not an operationalizable mechanism for software engineering organizations.
Technical transpilation of EU AI Act logging and human oversight duties into computable clauses is inherently software-oriented and directly operationalizable in compliance automation pipelines.
Compliance framework explicitly structured around development and deployment lifecycle phases is directly applicable to software engineering organizations building AI systems.
Names operationalizable backdoor detection tools (Neural Cleanse, STRIP) for model validation audits, but one-sentence abstract prevents confirming methodological depth beyond tool enumeration.

Gap analysis identifies what ISO/IEC 42001 and EU AI Act lack for agentic AI but provides no operational mechanisms to fill those gaps — diagnostic for policymakers, not prescriptive for engineering orgs.
Paper explicitly states it is 'descriptive, not normative' and defers governance framework to a companion paper; evidence layer documentation without actionable guidance.

Under/over-reliance patterns and trust calibration findings in AI-assisted coding transfer to AI code review; the human-AI collaboration dynamic (review suggestions, accept/reject/modify) is structurally parallel.
Macro regulatory economics arguing automated compliance loosens innovation-regulation trade-offs 'under plausible assumptions' — theoretical argument, no implementation mechanism.
Critical analysis of HITL as rhetorical shield in federal rulemaking; meta-commentary on policy discourse provides no operational mechanism for meaningful oversight.
Explicitly sector-agnostic multi-layer validation architecture aligned with NIST AI RMF is designed for cross-domain transfer; software deployment in high-stakes environments fits the stated scope.

Five-layer governance framework claims production validation for agentic AI, but 'survival maximization' objective function and axiomatic derivation raise questions about practical operationalizability vs theoretical contribution.
Research proposal ('This research aims to...') describing intended methodology for wearable cognitive load measurement; no completed findings or framework yet available to operationalize.
Legal copyright analysis establishing that code memorization creates IP risk; characterizes the problem but provides no detection methodology or governance mechanism software orgs can implement.

Policy prototyping program explicitly addresses operationalizing EU AI Act human oversight requirements through experimental implementation with companies; practical compliance insights for regulatory planning.
No abstract available; title indicates general ethics guideline development without apparent operational mechanism for software engineering code governance.

Architectural patterns (tamper-evident decision ledgers, hallucination anchoring, attribute-based agent access control) are technical mechanisms transferable to software AI governance; financial domain is application context not the contribution.

Editorial synthesizing existing literature on oversight feasibility (XAI, HITL, regulatory approaches) and their limitations; life sciences anchor and survey format yield no new operational methodology.

HITL supervision, data lineage, and model-risk management patterns are governance mechanisms that transfer without substantial re-derivation; investment domain specificity is in examples not framework structure.
